key_points: Motion is described relative to a reference point and depends on the observer
Distance is a scalar quantity while displacement is a vector quantity
Average speed is calculated using total distance divided by total time
Average velocity is calculated using total displacement divided by total time
Acceleration can occur due to change in speed or change in direction of motion
Uniform acceleration occurs when velocity changes by equal amounts in equal time intervals
Retardation or negative acceleration occurs when velocity decreases with time
The slope of a distance–time graph gives the speed of the object
The slope of a velocity–time graph gives acceleration
The area under a velocity–time graph represents displacement
examples: Example of uniform motion: Train moving at constant speed on a straight track
Example of non-uniform motion: A car moving in city traffic
Example of circular motion: A satellite orbiting Earth
Example of acceleration due to direction change: Motion of a stone tied to a string
Example of retardation: A car slowing down when brakes are applied
Example of graph interpretation: Straight line distance–time graph indicating constant speed
formulas: Average Speed = Total Distance / Total Time
Average Velocity = Total Displacement / Total Time
exam_tips: Always identify whether the question involves distance or displacement
Remember that velocity includes direction while speed does not
In circular motion the speed may remain constant but velocity changes
Check the slope carefully when interpreting motion graphs
Use the correct equation of motion depending on the known variables
